title: The World Remembers
subtitle: The first history of the world, told the way the oldest texts tell it
intro: Every people on earth — separated by oceans they could not cross and centuries they could not share — wrote down the same handful of stories. A garden and a fall. Watchers from the sky. Giants in the land. A world drowned. An age that collapsed. A tower that broke the world's one language apart. They could not have copied one another. So why do they all remember the same things? Turn the page and watch the pattern rise.
motifKey: Each strip on a source card is a recurring story-element. When the same strip glows on cards from peoples who never met, you're looking at a shared memory — not a coincidence.

chapters.teaser: Before the Garden: water, darkness, a formless deep — and a power that divides it into light and dark, sky and sea. Creation as the ordering of chaos, told from Israel to Babylon to Egypt to India.
A paradise, a tree of life, a serpent, a forbidden choice — and immortality lost. The same shape from Eden to Sumer to Greece to the far north.
Beings came down from the sky, took human wives, and taught humanity what it wasn't meant to know — and the world began to go wrong.
Their children were giants — and peoples who never met describe the same towering, six-fingered, red-haired rulers, and the long war to be rid of them.
A god warns one man; he builds a vessel, saves the seed of life, sends out birds. Written this way on every inhabited continent by people who could not have copied one another.
Four civilizations on three continents collapsed at once, and the earth's own climate record agrees: an age ended.
One people, one language, a tower to heaven — and the breaking apart into the nations of the world.
A storm-god or hero fights the great serpent of the sea, and from that victory the ordered world is made or kept — from Babylon to Canaan to India to the north.
A divine figure goes down into death and comes back — and with him the grain, the spring, and the promise that death is not the end.
Where the texts meet the dirt: the steles, seals, tunnels and destruction layers that name the people and confirm the events.
The clay, papyrus and parchment that carried it all down — the libraries and the caves, and where you can still find them.
